introLines.text: Vern, I'm a retired Air Force pilot, and what I saw last week has kept me up every night since.
Twenty years flying fighters. I've seen everything that's supposed to be in our skies. This wasn't any of it.
I signed NDAs when I left the service. I'm breaking them tonight because people need to know.

detailLines.text: Radar contact at 40,000 feet, no transponder, performed maneuvers that would kill any human pilot. Command told us to forget it happened.
We tracked it for eighteen minutes. It descended from 80,000 feet to sea level in under four seconds. Nothing we have can do that.
The craft was disc-shaped, metallic, approximately 40 feet in diameter. I was close enough to see the seams.

defenseLines.text: They can discredit me all they want. I have my flight logs, I have witnesses. This happened.
I've got nothing to gain from this and everything to lose. Why would I make it up?
You think I want to be called crazy? I'm risking my pension for this.

acceptanceLines.text: That's exactly right. They don't want us talking about this.
Finally, someone who understands what's at stake here.
You're one of the few who gets it, Vern.

extraDetailLines.text: After we landed, men in suits were waiting on the tarmac. They took our recorders, our notes. Told us the mission never happened.
I've talked to pilots from three other bases. Same story. Same craft. Same orders to stay quiet.
The radar data was scrubbed within hours. But I kept a personal log. Old habit from my training days.

extraDefenseLines.text: I've got copies of everything stored in three different locations. If something happens to me, it all goes public.
My service record is spotless. Thirty years of distinguished service. I didn't throw that away to tell a story.
Two of my wingmen are prepared to corroborate. They're just waiting for the right moment.

conclusionLines.text: Keep doing what you're doing, Vern. The truth needs to get out.
I've said my piece. Do with it what you will.
